raw_case:
A rare case of intratumoral hemorrhage in a young adult with adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma
Craniopharyngiomas are rare, slow growing tumors arising along the craniopharyngeal duct. The incidence of craniopharyngioma was 0.13 per 100,000 persons per year. Intratumoral hemorrhage is a serious complication that can mimic pituitary tumor apoplexy, a life-threatening condition. In this report, We presented a case of a 20-year-old male with 3 days of decreased consciousness, with a history of worsening headaches, vomiting, blurry vision, bitemporal hemianopia, and right-sided limb weakness. MRI findings revealed a mixed cystic and solid suprasellar mass with blooming artifacts and fluid-fluid levels on SWI strongly suggest craniopharyngiomas with intratumoral hemorrhage. Trans-petrosal surgery was performed, the lesion appeared intraoperatively as a firm, elastic mass with a hemorrhagic component. Further histopathological testing confirmed the diagnosis of craniopharyngioma with adamantinomatous subtype, which typically occurs in children and adults over 45. While in this patient's diagnosis at age 20 falls outside the usual range. This study highlights the possibility of adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma occurring in young adulthood and the role of imaging in diagnosing craniopharyngioma, including its detailed characteristics and the presence of intratumoral hemorrhage for early management and better patient outcome.

Introduction

Craniopharyngiomas are rare malformation tumors of low-grade epithelial neoplasms that arise in the suprasellar region of the brain along the craniopharyngeal duct [1]. The incidence of craniopharyngioma was 0.13 per 100,000 persons per year and did not vary by gender or race [2]. There are 2 histological phenotypes seen in craniopharyngioma, adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma (ACP) and papillary craniopharyngioma (PCP), which differ in genesis and age distribution. ACPs occur in 2 age peaks (5-15 and 45-60 years), while PCPs predominantly affect adults in their fifth and sixth decades of life [1].Intratumoral hemorrhage within a craniopharyngioma is a rare but severe complication. The clinical manifestation of acute hemorrhage in craniopharyngioma, especially for intrasellar craniopharyngioma can mimic pituitary tumor apoplexy, such as the acute onset of visual disorders and hormonal deficiency. When the hematoma blocks up the intraventricular foramen or enters the third ventricle with protrusion of the tumor, expansion of the ventricular system and hydrocephalus can appear [3].This case report presents a 20-year-old male with craniopharyngioma complicated by intratumoral hemorrhage and the role of imaging for the diagnosis and management of craniopharyngioma.

Case presentation

We presented a case of a 20-year-old male with 3 days of decreased consciousness preceded by a history of headaches since 2 months ago that worsened significantly in the past week. The headaches were constant, pressure-like throughout the head with sharp left-sided pain radiating to the upper left face. He also experienced vomiting, blurry vision, and bitemporal hemianopia for 2 months. He reported right-sided limb weakness but remained ambulatory and independent. Facial asymmetry leftward deviation was noted (Fig. 1). No sensory loss or slurred speech was observed.MRI demonstrated a multilobulated mixed mass in the suprasellar region with solid, cystic, hemorrhagic, and calcified components. Size of mass is about 6.01 × 6.81 × 7.54 cm. Mass compresses optic nerves, optic chiasm, left lateral ventricles, third and fourth ventricle, left basal ganglia, left temporal lobe, brainstem, and extends to infratentorially and compresses the medulla oblongata. Causes subfalcine herniation with displacement of midline structures up to 1.1 cm to the right. Blooming artifacts on susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) with fluid-fluid levels suggest intratumoral hemorrhage (Fig. 2).The patient underwent a craniotomy for tumor removal using an anterior trans petrosal approach. The intraoperative appearance of the lesion was that of a hard-elastic mass with hemorrhagic component (Fig. 3). The mass was sent for histopathological examination, confirming adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma in the suprasellar and pontine regions. Microscopy reveals mixed solid and cystic epithelial tumors with stellate reticulum, wet keratin and blood clot (blood clot not revealed in picture) (Fig. 4).Intraoperative findings revealed that the tumor adhesion to the basilar artery, so complete resection was not possible. Postoperative head CT revealed a residual tumor in the suprasellar region (Fig. 5). After surgery, the patient's consciousness has improved, but they are still experiencing mild headache, left visual field loss, and no longer feel weakness in the right-sided limb. The patient is scheduled for a follow-up appointment to determine the course of further treatment.

Discussion

Craniopharyngiomas are rare malformational tumors of low grade epithelial neoplasms that arise in the suprasellar region of the brain along the craniopharyngeal duct [1]. The incidence of craniopharyngioma was 0.13 per 100,000 persons per year and did not vary by gender or race [2]. Based on data obtained from Hasan Sadikin Hospital in Bandung, the number of craniopharyngioma cases over the past 5 years (2020-2024) reached 3 cases, one of these cases were accompanied by intratumoral hemorrhage. There are 2 histological phenotypes seen in craniopharyngioma, adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma (ACP) and papillary craniopharyngioma (PCP), which differ in genesis and age distribution. ACPs occur in 2 age peaks (5-15 and 45-60 years), while PCPs predominantly affect adults in their fifth and sixth decades of life [1].Craniopharyngiomas arise from cellular remnants of the Rathke pouch, which is an embryologic structure forming the infundibulum and anterior lobe of the pituitary gland. The possibility of persistent embryonic cells from Rathke's pouch could be the source of tumor origin in adults. This explains their frequent occurrence in suprasellar, parasellar, and even less common ectopic locations [4]. These tumors can arise in various locations, purely intrasellar-infra diaphragmatic (Type a), intrasellar extending suprasellar, infra- and supradiaphragmatic (Type b), supradiaphragmatic, parachiasmatic, extra- ventricular (Type c), intra- and extraventricular (Type d), paraventricular (Type e), purely intraventricular (Type f), or rarely, epidural (middle fossa) or extracranial [5].Acute clinical presentation of craniopharyngioma includes sudden headache, nausea, vomiting, cranial nerves palsy, visual deficits (diplopia, bitemporal hemianopsia, and other visual symptoms), diabetes insipidus, fever, or reduced level of consciousness. Within the different types of acute presentation, intratumoral haemorrhage is one of the most uncommon [6]. Intratumoral hemorrhage within a craniopharyngioma is a rare but severe complication. The clinical manifestation of acute hemorrhage in craniopharyngioma, especially for intrasellar craniopharyngioma can mimic pituitary tumor apoplexy, such as the acute onset of visual disorders and hormonal deficiency. When the hematoma blocks up the intraventricular foramen or enters the third ventricle with protrusion of the tumor, expansion of the ventricular system and hydrocephalus can appear [3].On imaging, craniopharyngioma is classified by size and its relationship to the optic chiasm. Large tumors can extend into the anterior, middle, or posterior cranial fossae or ventricles. Their relationship to the optic chiasm, hypothalamus, third ventricle, and arteries of the circle of Willis is crucial for surgical planning [7]. Modern imaging technologies such as computed tomography (CT) and magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) provide detailed imaging for diagnosing and managing craniopharyngioma.CT scans reveal calcifications in up to 90% of pediatric craniopharyngiomas. These tumors typically appear hypodense on CT due to their mixed cystic and solid components. A CT scan can also illustrate secondary skull bases changes such as enlargement of the sella turcica or erosion of the dorsum sellae or reveal important calcifications that assist in making a diagnosis. These potential alterations in the skull base anatomy are important to note when planning a surgical approach [8].Critical information about the tumor and its surrounding neurovascular structures is best seen on MRI. Detailed evaluation of the location of the optic nerves and chiasm, pituitary gland and stalk, hypothalamus, circle of Willis vascular anatomy, brainstem, frontal and temporal lobes, and ventricles. Cystic components typically appear homogeneously hyperintense on T2-WI, and generally hypointense but can vary on T1-WI. Solid portions will have similar appearance as other brain tumors with iso- or hypointense signal on T1-WI, and mixed signal intensity on T2-WI. Enhancement after contrast is common for both cystic and solid components. Calcifications may be missed on MRI due to potential confusion with flow voids. Susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI), a specialized MRI technique, is highly sensitive to blood products. The resulting blooming artifacts and fluid-fluid levels on SWI strongly suggest intratumoral hemorrhage, especially when fresh blood appears hyperintense on T1-weighted scans. Magnetic resonance angiography (MRA) can assess the tumor's relation to critical vascular structures. Magnetic resonance spectroscopy (MRS) can identify characteristic elevated peaks of lactate or lipids, with only small amounts of other metabolites. This can help differentiate them from gliomas and pituitary adenomas [8]. On MRS, craniopharyngiomas demonstrate characteristic lactate or lipid peaks, with only small amounts of other metabolites, whereas gliomas typically demonstrate choline, n-acetyl aspartate, and creatine peaks, and pituitary adenomas demonstrate choline peaks or no elevated metabolites [9].According to the International Classification of Diseases for Oncology (ICD-O), adamantinomatous craniopharyngiomas have a behavior code of /1, indicating unspecified, borderline, or uncertain behavior [10]. Adamantinomatous craniopharyngiomas contain solid and cystic components with heterogeneous tumor epithelium bordering a layer of stellate reticulum cells and nodules of “wet keratin” formed by nuclear squamous cells and frequently exhibit regressive changes like cholesterol clefts, calcification, and hemosiderin deposits. These features are hallmarks of adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma tumors. Papillary craniopharyngiomas consist of well-differentiated squamous epithelium with goblet and ciliated cells, they rarely calcify and lack peripheral palisading, stellate reticulum cells, and wet keratin [11].The differential diagnosis of craniopharyngioma includes congenital anomalies (Arachnoid cyst and Rathke's cleft cyst); Other Tumors (Pituitary tumor, metastasis, meningioma, epidermoid and dermoid tumor, hypothalamic-optic pathway glioma, hypothalamic hamartoma, teratoma); Infectious/Inflammatory processes (Eosinophilic granuloma, lymphocytic hypophysitis, sarcoidosis, syphilis, and tuberculosis); Vascular malformations (aneurysm of the internal carotid or anterior communicating artery, arterio-venous malformation) [12].Craniopharyngioma management primarily involves surgery or combined surgery and radiation. Traditional open surgical approaches, including pterional, orbitozygomatic, sub-frontal, frontobasal interhemispheric, transpetrosal, and transcallosal-transventricular approaches, have been used to resect craniopharyngioma [5,13,14]. In recent years, endoscopic endonasal surgery (EES) is a more recent option for suprasellar craniopharyngiomas at many institutions. Gross total resection (GTR) is the optimal surgical goal for craniopharyngioma due to superior progression-free survival (PFS) rate compared to subgross total resection (STR) [15,16]. Preserving hypothalamic function is crucial to prevent long-term complications, such as endocrine dysfunction, cognitive ability decline, and hypothalamic obesity. For cases in which complete tumor removal is impossible, subgross total resection (STR) followed by radiotherapy can effectively manage the disease. However, injury to endocrine function and the hypothalamus still needs to be addressed. CSF leakage is one of the most concerning complications [17]. Due to GTR and STR combined with radiotherapy having similar effects on the 5- and 10-year PFS or overall survival of CP, some surgeons favor limited surgical interventions combined with radiotherapy to minimize hypothalamic damage, especially in elderly patients or those with complex tumor adherence to the basal ganglia or the inferior surfaces of the hypothalamus [13].The prognosis of patients with craniopharyngiomas is much less favorable than that of patients with other benign extra-axial epithelial tumors due to craniopharyngiomas having a high propensity for recurrence rates and damage to vital structures. Repeated surgeries and treatments can significantly impact patient health [18].In this case, we presented a 20-year-old male with craniopharyngioma complicated by intratumoral hemorrhage. This is a rare case due to the incidence is low. Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma (ACP) subtype typically presents in children and older adults unlike the patient. MRI findings in this patient are consistent with a diagnosis of craniopharyngioma, due to their mixed cystic and solid components, with some showing blooming artifacts on susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) with fluid-fluid levels suggesting intratumoral hemorrhage. The diagnosis of craniopharyngioma is further supported by the findings of the pathological examination. Early trans-petrosal surgery was performed within 2 weeks after MRI, which resulted in moderate neurological recovery.

Conclusion

Craniopharyngiomas with intratumoral hemorrhage are considered rare among craniopharyngioma cases. The tumor in our patient was unusual as it occurred in a young adulthood. The clinical manifestation of acute hemorrhage in craniopharyngioma, especially for intrasellar craniopharyngioma can mimic pituitary tumor apoplexy which is a potentially life-threatening condition. Even though the confirmed diagnosis was made by histopathological examination, preoperative imaging examinations help to determine the location, assess the mass characteristics, and choose the appropriate treatment options. The results of early Trans-petrosal surgery procedures are satisfactory than patients who operated later.

Patient consent

The patient has seen a version of the manuscript to be submitted/published and he gave his consent for his image or other information relating to him to be reported in the above-named manuscript for consideration of publication in the Radiology Case Reports (RCR).The patient understands that protected health information such as identification number, billing information, and address, will not be published and that efforts will be made to conceal his identity. However, diagnostic or medical imaging may be published.The patient understands that the material may be published in the Radiology Case Reports (RCR) Journal. As a result, he understands that the material may be seen by the general public. He understands that he may revoke consent at any time before publication, but once the information has been published revocation of the consent is no longer possible.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 20-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Decreased consciousness for 3 days
- **History of Present Illness:**  
  - 2 months of worsening headaches, pressure-like throughout the head with sharp left-sided pain radiating to the upper left face.  
  - Associated symptoms include vomiting, blurry vision, bitemporal hemianopia, and right-sided limb weakness (ambulatory and independent).  
  - Severe headache exacerbated over the past week.
- **Past Medical History:** None
- **Personal History:** None
- **Family History:** None
- **Physical Examination:**  
  - Decreased consciousness  
  - Leftward facial asymmetry without sensory loss or slurred speech
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **MRI findings:** Mixed cystic and solid mass in the suprasellar region with hemorrhagic and calcified components (6.01 × 6.81 × 7.54 cm) compressing the optic nerves, chiasm, ventricles, and left basal ganglia. Subfalcine herniation observed with midline shift of 1.1 cm. Blooming artifacts and fluid-fluid levels on susceptibility-weighted imaging (SWI) suggest intratumoral hemorrhage.  
  - **Histopathology:** Confirmation of adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma with hallmarks, including stellate reticulum, wet keratin formation, and mixed solid/cystic tumor structure.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
1. **Initial Considerations:**  
   - The combination of decreased consciousness, bitemporal hemianopia, worsening headaches, and MRI-detected suprasellar mass raised suspicion for brain tumors or other space-occupying lesions.  
   - Common differentials included **pituitary tumor apoplexy**, **craniopharyngioma**, **Rathke's cleft cyst**, **hypothalamic glioma**, and rarer entities such as **vascular malformation** or **infection/inflammation (lymphocytic hypophysitis)**.

2. **Rationale for Inclusion/Exclusion of Each Condition:**  
   - **Pituitary tumor apoplexy:** Ruled out due to clear imaging differentiation, as craniopharyngiomas show mixed cystic/solid components with calcification, which is uncommon in pituitary adenomas. Histopathology provided no adenomatous evidence.  
   - **Rathke's cleft cyst:** Excluded because Rathke's cleft cysts typically lack solid or calcified components and do not show the characteristic stellate reticulum or wet keratin seen histologically in this case.  
   - **Hypothalamic glioma:** MRI appearances of craniopharyngiomas differ from gliomas, as malignant gliomas lack calcified components and the characteristic histopathological features of adamantinomatous craniopharyngiomas.  
   - **Vascular malformation (e.g., aneurysm or AVM):** Excluded based on imaging, as no vascular abnormalities such as flow voids or typical enhancement patterns of malformations were observed.  
   - **Craniopharyngioma:** Supported by the imaging findings of cystic and solid components, calcification, hemorrhage, and the clinical presentation. Definitively confirmed by histopathology with hallmark features.  

3. **Stepwise Diagnostic Refinement:**  
   - Radiological findings initially narrowed differentials to craniopharyngioma, pituitary adenoma, or Rathke’s cleft cyst.  
   - The presence of blooming artifacts on SWI and wet keratin on histopathology were pivotal for excluding non-hemorrhagic and non-craniopharyngioma entities.  
   - Adamantinomatous subtype was diagnosed following histopathological confirmation.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma with intratumoral hemorrhage, complicated by mass effect causing subfalcine herniation.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> Analyze the Chief Complaint and History of Present Illness: The patient is a 20-year-old male presenting with decreased consciousness for 3 days.  Prior to this acute deterioration, he experienced a 2-month history of worsening headaches, described as pressure-like throughout the head with sharp left-sided pain radiating to the upper left face.  Associated symptoms include vomiting, blurry vision, bitemporal hemianopia, and right-sided limb weakness. The headache severity significantly increased in the week leading to decreased consciousness. These symptoms suggest a progressive intracranial lesion affecting the visual pathways (bitemporal hemianopia), motor pathways (right-sided weakness), and causing increased intracranial pressure (headaches, vomiting, decreased consciousness).

<step 2> Review Past Medical, Personal, and Family History: The patient has no significant past medical, personal, or family history. This reduces the likelihood of systemic diseases or genetic predispositions influencing the current condition, pointing towards a primary neurological pathology.

<step 3> Analyze Physical Examination Findings: The physical examination reveals decreased consciousness and leftward facial asymmetry. Decreased consciousness is a critical finding indicating severe neurological compromise.  Leftward facial asymmetry, without sensory loss or slurred speech, might suggest upper motor neuron facial weakness, potentially related to the intracranial lesion.

<step 4> Critically Evaluate Ancillary Tests - MRI Findings: The MRI is highly informative. It reveals a large (6.01 × 6.81 × 7.54 cm) mixed cystic and solid mass in the suprasellar region. This location is highly suggestive of lesions affecting the pituitary gland, hypothalamus, and optic chiasm.  The presence of hemorrhagic and calcified components is also significant.  Critically, the mass is compressing vital structures: optic nerves, optic chiasm (explaining bitemporal hemianopia), ventricles (likely causing obstructive hydrocephalus and contributing to increased intracranial pressure and vomiting), and left basal ganglia (explaining right-sided limb weakness).  The observation of subfalcine herniation with a midline shift of 1.1 cm is a serious finding, indicating significant intracranial pressure and potential for further neurological deterioration and decreased consciousness. Blooming artifacts and fluid-fluid levels on SWI are consistent with intratumoral hemorrhage, which could explain the recent exacerbation of symptoms and decreased consciousness.

<step 5> Critically Evaluate Ancillary Tests - Histopathology: Histopathology confirms the diagnosis as adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma. The characteristic hallmarks like stellate reticulum, wet keratin formation, and mixed solid/cystic tumor structure are explicitly mentioned, solidifying this diagnosis. Craniopharyngiomas are known to occur in the suprasellar region and can present with the symptoms described, including visual disturbances, headaches, and endocrine dysfunction (although endocrine dysfunction is not explicitly mentioned in the provided summary, it is a common association).

<step 6> Synthesize all findings and arrive at the Final Diagnosis:  Integrating all the clinical presentation (progressive headaches, visual symptoms, limb weakness, decreased consciousness), radiological findings (suprasellar mass with compression of vital structures, hemorrhage, calcification, herniation), and histopathological confirmation (adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma), the final diagnosis is definitively established as adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma with significant mass effect and complications including intratumoral hemorrhage and subfalcine herniation, leading to decreased consciousness.

### Answer:
Adamantinomatous Craniopharyngioma

Prior to this acute deterioration, he experienced a 2-month history of worsening headaches, described as pressure-like throughout the head with sharp left-sided pain radiating to the upper left face.  Associated symptoms include vomiting, blurry vision, bitemporal hemianopia, and right-sided limb weakness. The headache severity significantly increased in the week leading to decreased consciousness. These symptoms suggest a progressive intracranial lesion affecting the visual pathways (bitemporal hemianopia), motor pathways (right-sided weakness), and causing increased intracranial pressure (headaches, vomiting, decreased consciousness).

Review Past Medical, Personal, and Family History: The patient has no significant past medical, personal, or family history. This reduces the likelihood of systemic diseases or genetic predispositions influencing the current condition, pointing towards a primary neurological pathology.

Analyze Physical Examination Findings: The physical examination reveals decreased consciousness and leftward facial asymmetry. Decreased consciousness is a critical finding indicating severe neurological compromise.  Leftward facial asymmetry, without sensory loss or slurred speech, might suggest upper motor neuron facial weakness, potentially related to the intracranial lesion.

Critically Evaluate Ancillary Tests - MRI Findings: The MRI is highly informative. It reveals a large (6.01 × 6.81 × 7.54 cm) mixed cystic and solid mass in the suprasellar region. This location is highly suggestive of lesions affecting the pituitary gland, hypothalamus, and optic chiasm.  The presence of hemorrhagic and calcified components is also significant.  Critically, the mass is compressing vital structures: optic nerves, optic chiasm (explaining bitemporal hemianopia), ventricles (likely causing obstructive hydrocephalus and contributing to increased intracranial pressure and vomiting), and left basal ganglia (explaining right-sided limb weakness).  The observation of subfalcine herniation with a midline shift of 1.1 cm is a serious finding, indicating significant intracranial pressure and potential for further neurological deterioration and decreased consciousness. Blooming artifacts and fluid-fluid levels on SWI are consistent with intratumoral hemorrhage, which could explain the recent exacerbation of symptoms and decreased consciousness.

Critically Evaluate Ancillary Tests - Histopathology: Histopathology confirms the diagnosis as adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma. The characteristic hallmarks like stellate reticulum, wet keratin formation, and mixed solid/cystic tumor structure are explicitly mentioned, solidifying this diagnosis. Craniopharyngiomas are known to occur in the suprasellar region and can present with the symptoms described, including visual disturbances, headaches, and endocrine dysfunction (although endocrine dysfunction is not explicitly mentioned in the provided summary, it is a common association).

Synthesize all findings and arrive at the Final Diagnosis:  Integrating all the clinical presentation (progressive headaches, visual symptoms, limb weakness, decreased consciousness), radiological findings (suprasellar mass with compression of vital structures, hemorrhage, calcification, herniation), and histopathological confirmation (adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma), the final diagnosis is definitively established as adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma with significant mass effect and complications including intratumoral hemorrhage and subfalcine herniation, leading to decreased consciousness.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: The combination of decreased consciousness, bitemporal hemianopia, worsening headaches, and MRI-detected suprasellar mass raised suspicion for brain tumors or other space-occupying lesions.

Common differentials included pituitary tumor apoplexy, craniopharyngioma, Rathke's cleft cyst, hypothalamic glioma, and rarer entities such as vascular malformation or infection/inflammation (lymphocytic hypophysitis).

Pituitary tumor apoplexy ruled out due to clear imaging differentiation, as craniopharyngiomas show mixed cystic/solid components with calcification, which is uncommon in pituitary adenomas. Histopathology provided no adenomatous evidence.

Rathke's cleft cyst excluded because Rathke's cleft cysts typically lack solid or calcified components and do not show the characteristic stellate reticulum or wet keratin seen histologically in this case.

Hypothalamic glioma MRI appearances of craniopharyngiomas differ from gliomas, as malignant gliomas lack calcified components and the characteristic histopathological features of adamantinomatous craniopharyngiomas.
Vascular malformation (e.g., aneurysm or AVM) excluded based on imaging, as no vascular abnormalities such as flow voids or typical enhancement patterns of malformations were observed.

Craniopharyngioma supported by the imaging findings of cystic and solid components, calcification, hemorrhage, and the clinical presentation. Definitively confirmed by histopathology with hallmark features.
Radiological findings initially narrowed differentials to craniopharyngioma, pituitary adenoma, or Rathke’s cleft cyst.
Presence of blooming artifacts on SWI and wet keratin on histopathology were pivotal for excluding non-hemorrhagic and non-craniopharyngioma entities.
Adamantinomatous subtype diagnosed following histopathological confirmation.
Final diagnosis: Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma with intratumoral hemorrhage, complicated by mass effect causing subfalcine herniation.
